ip_outcome_note: Card name 'Dunsparce', Colorless type, Basic subtype, Mysterious Nest ability. National Pokedex no. 206, Johto Land Snake Pokemon. Confirmed.

trivia_bullets.text: Dunsparce (no. 206) debuted Generation II (Gold/Silver, 1999) and did not receive an evolution for 23 years — the longest evolutionary gap of any Pokemon that subsequently gained one. Dudunsparce (no. 982) arrived in Generation IX (Scarlet/Violet, 2022), requiring the move Hyper Drill (level 32). Standard result is two-segment form; three-segment form exists at 1-in-100 odds.
sources: Nintendo Life: How to Evolve Dunsparce into Dudunsparce

trivia_bullets.text: "When it sees a person, it digs a hole with its tail to make its escape. If you happen to find one, consider yourself lucky." The rarity framing ('consider yourself lucky') echoes Dunsparce's in-game reputation as a hard-to-find encounter, feeding decades of community jokes about its lack of evolution.
Mysterious Nest ability (this print): removes Weakness from all Colorless Pokemon in play for both sides. A niche effect for a Pokemon characterized by evasiveness. [PokemonTCG.io: swsh8-207]

Dunsparce is based on the tsuchinoko, a cryptid from Japanese folklore — a fat-bodied snake creature of near-mythological rarity, reportedly sighted in Japanese mountain regions. The tsuchinoko connection explains the 'if you find one, consider yourself lucky' flavor text and the species' in-game reputation as an elusive mid-game encounter.

related_cards.name: Dudunsparce
related_cards.note: The 23-years-later evolution, two- and three-segment forms
